name: Nazi Flying Saucers
description: The Nazi Flying Saucers theory proposes that Nazi Germany, during World War II, researched and developed advanced flying saucer-like aircraft or even functional extraterrestrial-derived technology. Proponents often claim that these technologies were either hidden away at the end of the war and later used by other nations, or continue to exist in secret underground bases, possibly in Antarctica or South America. The origins of the theory are murky, appearing in the post-war period alongside the rise of UFO sightings and fueled by speculation about German technological advancements during the war, such as the V-2 rocket and unusual aircraft designs. Key evidence cited typically includes anecdotal accounts, purported leaked documents (often of dubious authenticity), and misinterpretations of historical aircraft projects. Mainstream historians and scientists dismiss the theory as pseudoscience and conspiracy theory. They point out the lack of credible evidence, the exaggeration of German technological capabilities during the war, and the absence of any confirmed sightings or physical evidence of advanced flying saucer technology originating from Nazi Germany. The theory often intersects with other fringe beliefs, including hollow Earth theories and claims of secret Nazi survival in remote locations. Its enduring appeal likely stems from a combination of fascination with UFOs, anxieties about hidden power structures, and the lingering mystique surrounding Nazi Germany's wartime technological ambitions.
subtitle: Alleged advanced aircraft of Nazi Germany

claims.claim: Nazi Germany developed functioning flying saucers during WWII.
claims.evidence: Alleged leaked documents, witness testimonies, and designs of experimental aircraft.
claims.criticism: Lack of credible documentation, unreliable witness accounts, misinterpretation of experimental aircraft projects, and absence of physical evidence.

claims.claim: These flying saucers were hidden after the war and either used by other nations or remain in secret bases.
claims.evidence: Speculation based on the lack of publicly available information and supposed sightings in remote areas.
claims.criticism: No verifiable evidence of hidden bases or recovered technology. Attributing UFO sightings to Nazi technology is speculative and lacks scientific basis.

claims.claim: The technology was extraterrestrial in origin and given to the Nazis.
claims.evidence: References to esoteric Nazi beliefs and alleged connections to ancient civilizations with advanced knowledge.
claims.criticism: These claims are based on pseudoscientific interpretations of history and lack any credible evidence of extraterrestrial contact or technology transfer.

keyFigures.name: Renato Vesco
keyFigures.role: Early proponent who popularized the theory in his book 'Intercept UFO'

keyFigures.name: Igor Witkowski
keyFigures.role: Polish author who wrote about the 'Nazi Bell' and alleged secret weapons programs.

keyFigures.name: Joseph P. Farrell
keyFigures.role: Author who connects Nazi technology with esoteric beliefs and ancient mysteries.

keyFigures.name: Michael Shermer
keyFigures.role: Science writer and skeptic who critiques conspiracy theories, including the Nazi flying saucer myth.

mythologyConnections: Connections to Germanic paganism and occultism

timeline.event: Rise of occult societies in Germany, such as the Thule Society and Vril Society, which are later linked to the theory.
Alleged Nazi research and development of advanced aircraft and flying saucer technology.
Emergence of UFO sightings and the popularization of the Nazi flying saucer theory in books and media.
Continued interest in the theory through documentaries, online forums, and alternative history communities.

sources.author: Joseph P. Farrell
sources.work: Reich of the Black Sun: Nazi Secret Weapons & the Cold War Allied Legend
sources.title: Joseph P. Farrell

sources.author: Robert Sheaffer
sources.work: Bad UFOs: Exposing the Myths and Lies Behind the Flying Saucer Phenomenon
sources.title: Robert Sheaffer
